infons.title: Keywords

passages.text: Physics-based simulation of ultrasound propagation from MRI volumes for cross-modal breast imaging
Background

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of breast tissue is often used to definitively diagnose breast cancer due to its high soft-tissue contrast. However, its high cost and limited accessibility make it unsuitable for real-time screening, unlike ultrasound. Machine learning models that synthesize MRI from ultrasound could enable low-cost screening and triage by providing MRI-like tissue characterization during routine ultrasound exams. However, machine learning models trained to generate synthetic MRIs from ultrasound often struggle due to the absence of paired datasets across imaging modalities. This lack of paired datasets is a particular struggle in the field of model building for medical imaging purposes. We propose a physics-based method for generating synthetic ultrasound images from MRI volumes using mathematical modeling of acoustic beam propagation and tissue composition.
Results

We use a singular MRI example as a proof-of-concept of this physics simulation, which is based on pre-existing ultrasound physics. Each MRI slice was divided into a 2 × 2 grid to identify local beam entry regions. Tangent lines were calculated along each grid edge to estimate perpendicular ultrasound beam paths in a pinwheel fashion around the breast tissue. Initial simulations indicate that the generated ultrasound-like images reproduce plausible intensity gradients and textural variation consistent with known acoustic-tissue interactions.
Conclusions

In resource-limited settings, access to MRI for breast cancer workup remains a significant barrier when ultrasound findings are inconclusive. To enable development of cross-modal synthesis models, we present a physics-based simulation framework that creates paired MRI-ultrasound training datasets by synthesizing realistic ultrasound images from available MRI data. This supports the feasibility of using physics-driven methods to generate synthetic ultrasound from MRI data. Because contrast enhancement and vascular permeability are not modeled, this framework should be interpreted as a non-contrast anatomical MRI to conventional B-mode ultrasound simulation rather than a complete cross-modal transformation that captures DCE-MRI or contrast-enhanced ultrasound behavior.
Background

Ultrasounds are routinely used in clinical practice as a first-line imaging technique. Oftentimes, this form of imaging is the only method that is the most accessible for patients, and the safest form of imaging in certain cases, such as in pregnancy. Because of this, point-of-care ultrasounds (POCUS) are an incredibly important tool for rapid and early testing of various conditions. In the case of breast cancer diagnosis, breast ultrasound is typically used in adjunct with mammogram imaging. Although ultrasound is not the gold standard in the diagnosis of breast cancer, it offers additional information in situations where the breast tissue is incredibly dense. Mammograms, which are the gold standard of diagnosis, traditionally struggle with dense tissue. This makes early detection using ultrasound of breast cancer in these individuals incredibly important. Using machine learning techniques to improve early detection in these patients could consist of generating synthetic MRIs and mammograms from ultrasound images. This would allow better diagnosis for patients with limited access to advanced imaging equipment. Limited data access is a major barrier to developing machine learning models. By constructing paired artificial datasets, we can build models for data-scarce scenarios, such as rare medical conditions where patient data is inherently limited. Breast cancer’s extensive imaging data makes it an ideal case study for developing artificial datasets, particularly for physics-driven solutions based on theoretical principles rather than generative approaches. Therefore, to develop these types of models, we must first create a large paired dataset of backgrounds to MRIs, over which the machine learning model can be trained.

The current landscape of MRI-to-ultrasound transformation is heavily focused on deep learning approaches, particularly convolutional neural networks (CNNs), transformer architectures, and multimodal feature fusion. These techniques excel at extracting high-dimensional representations from imaging data and have shown promising results in segmentation and classification tasks. However, deep learning models lack interpretability, making it difficult to understand the basis of their decisions. This is especially problematic in clinical settings where transparency is paramount for provider trust and regulatory approval. These models also often require large, labeled datasets for training. In many cases, especially with paired MRI and ultrasound data, such datasets are limited or entirely unavailable, hindering the development of reliable models. Even if the datasets are publicly available, most models do not reveal source code, limiting reproducibility. While feature fusion between MRI and ultrasound data is theoretically appealing, differences in physics and tissue response between modalities pose significant challenges in training robust models that generalize well. In contrast, our method offers a physics-informed alternative that builds upon mathematical modeling of ultrasound propagation, inspired by prior work in ultrasound transformation from CT scans. Rather than relying on learned correlations, we use threshold-based contour detection and directional beam simulation to synthetically generate ultrasound-like images from MRI slices. This approach reduces the need for large training datasets and offers interpretability grounded in physical principles. Furthermore, it allows for precise control and validation of image generation behavior.

By demonstrating the feasibility of this method on a single representative image, we establish a foundational pipeline that can be further extended with variable tissue properties, attenuation models, or used to inform hybrid deep learning models with built-in physical priors. This work contributes a methodologically rigorous and interpretable baseline for future developments in cross-modality imaging synthesis. Our approach generates forward-modeled synthetic ultrasound images that can serve as training data for deep learning architectures.

A key reason breast MRI achieves high diagnostic performance is the use of dynamic contrast enhancement (DCE) with gadolinium-based contrast agents, which highlights tumor-associated angiogenesis and increased vascular permeability. Conventional ultrasound B-mode does not directly encode these vascular kinetics, which is the reason why MRI is often considered the next step after ultrasound is conducted. Accordingly, the present study targets a more limited but practical goal: generating ultrasound-like B-mode appearances from non-contrast anatomical MRI, without attempting to reproduce contrast-agent enhancement or blood-pool dynamics.

In addition, the development of this physics-informed model enables the generation of synthetic ultrasound images that can later be used to train or fine-tune CNN-based models. Similar research on these types of models bridges the gap between interpretable modeling and data-driven learning by embedding physical constraints into neural network training. This improves generalization and provides a framework for explainable artificial intelligence in medical imaging. Other studies have focused on abdominal ultrasound simulation, band cardiac models, but very little physics simulations exist for breast ultrasound. By creating a base dataset rooted in physical principles, we ensure that the resulting MRI-to-ultrasound transformations retain clinical validity and anatomical coherence. This approach not only bridges the gap between interpretable modeling and data-driven learning, but also offers a means of augmenting limited paired datasets, thereby enhancing the reliability and generalizability of downstream machine learning models.
Methods
Dataset

The breast MRIs that were used in this study were sourced from the Duke Breast Cancer MRI Dataset, specifically the pre-contrast phase of a DCE-MRI acquisition. This dataset, openly available from the National Cancer Institute, comprises of a 10-year single-institutional, retrospective collection of 922 invasive breast cancer patients. To demonstrate the feasibility of our mathematical approach, we applied the method to a single representative MRI volume. This allowed for isolated analysis of the method’s behavior, free from dataset-induced variability. Future work will expand to large-scale datasets to validate generalizability.
Data pre-processing
The MRI volume was separated into slices in the X-Y plane, resulting in multiple images at varying depths of the volume. The volume was then cropped to only include one breast.
Segmentation of the image

We used two different approaches to segment the MRI slice before determining the breast’s contour edges. The goal was to divide the 2D image into smaller ultrasound-like regions that could later be stitched together. Here, we partitioned the image into square sections using a 2 × 2 grid.
(Left) MRI slice intensity after applying normalization. (Right) MRI slice intensity with the Otsu’s thresholding value applied

To approximate the boundary of the breast tissue within a 2D MRI slice, we employed a multi-step image processing pipeline based on Otsu’s thresholding and directional edge tracing. Distributions of this thresholding is shown in Fig. 1. Otsu’s thresholding is a global binarization technique that allows us to automatically separate the foreground from the background in a grayscale image. We use this method to automatically determine an optimal threshold value for separating foreground breast tissue from background noise.

First, we normalized the MRI slice to the range [0, 255], after which Otsu’s algorithm computed a threshold value of 68.0 for this representative slice by maximizing between-class variance. This threshold was used to create a binary mask where tissue regions were marked as white (255) and background as black (0). In multi-slice applications, this threshold would be recomputed for each slice independently to account for depth-dependent intensity variations.

To extract a contour-like representation of the tissue boundary, we traced three key edges from the binary mask: the left edge, the top edge, and the right edge. To detect the left edge, For each row (y), the first non-zero pixel from the left was recorded. To detect the top edge, for each column (x), the first non-zero pixel from the top was recorded. And for the right edge, for each row (y), the last non-zero pixel from the right was recorded. These points were then concatenated in the order left, top, right, to construct an approximate outer contour of the tissue region. The resulting contour points were overlaid on the original normalized image to confirm the accuracy of boundary extraction, and is shown in Fig. 2.
Contour detection using Otsu’s thresholding of the breast

We then divided the breast into four quadrants, calculating the tangent line in each quadrant. This tangent line was calculated to determine the tissue orientation within each quadrant. To do this, we fit a smooth contour line to the segmented points and calculated a tangent vector (Fig. 3). We created a parametric B-spline curve through the detected contour points of each tile. For each selected tile, we sampled 100 points along the spline and calculated the tangent line at each point. An example of this tangent line calculation is shown in Fig. 3. The first derivative of the spline at this point provided the direction of the curve, which was then normalized to a unit vector. These local tangents provide the direction of simulated ultrasound beam propagation, ensuring that the generated images reflect realistic tissue geometry and orientation.
Tangent line vectors as determined by tile region
Overview of the simulator

Our model is a B-mode simulator that maps the MRI image by following a simplified pulse–echo chain. We attempt to imitate the main steps of an ultrasound machine by sending short sound pulses along many synthetic beams. At each tissue boundary, we collect echoes that bounce back. In order to accurately capture the tissue type changes, we binned the MRI into nine intensity classes, where each class corresponds to a tissue type. To assign these bins, we grouped MRI pixel intensities into nine ranges so that similar-looking tissues were treated the same way. The number of bins was determined empirically through histogram analysis of the MRI dataset. We first looked at the image histogram, found the natural breaks, and set fixed cut points from dark to bright. Darker bins capture fat-rich tissue; middle bins capture fibroglandular and stromal tissue; the brightest bins capture very dense structures such as fascia or calcification. Each bin was then assigned a reasonable acoustic impedance and attenuation taken from published ranges: fat gets lower values, fibroglandular and muscle get moderate values, and very dense tissue gets higher values. These bins are summarized in Table 1. We kept the choices mid-range to avoid overfitting and used the same bins for all experiments for reproducibility. When generalized for future studies, these cut points should be re-estimated once for the local MRI sequence and then kept fixed.
Tissue classification and acoustic properties

Table 1 describes the MRI intensity-to-acoustic property mapping. For bins corresponding to established tissue types (adipose, fibroglandular, muscle, bone), acoustic properties were drawn from published literature. Intermediate bins (3–4, 7–8) were assigned values that maintain smooth gradients between these anchor tissues to prevent artificial intensity discontinuities in the simulated ultrasound.

Each tissue class was assigned two acoustic properties: acoustic impedance (Z)(MRayl) and a frequency-dependent attenuation (𝛼) (dB/cm/MHz). The acoustic impedance gives information on the tissue boundary contrast, and is the product of density and the speed of sound. Attenuation gives information on shadowing and enhancement of the tissues. This allows for us to account for the loss of amplitude with depth when propagating waves through tissue. Together, these two properties define the majority of the physics involved in ultrasound.

Using these two properties yields spatial maps Z(x) and 𝛼(x). Acoustic impedance for soft tissue typically lies around 1.4 to 1.7 MRayl. Because of this, bins representing adipose, fibroglandular tissue, and muscle were assigned values within or near this interval. Higher impedances were reserved for high-intensity inclusions (such as calcifications), for which bone-like values were taken from the literature. For gel, we use MRayl and  dB/cm/MHz.

For the attenuation, we define it to be  for fat-like to fibroglandular-like tissues, which are values that previous literature have defined it to be. This is because adipose tissue is relatively transparent to ultrasound, allowing for the ultrasound echoes to persist with depth. For fibroglandular tissue, the higher attenuation is due to the ability of this tissue to scatter much more energy. This produces progressive darkening with depth, and stronger shadowing, leading to this higher attenuation value. This is important because it is this difference in tissue mapping that allows us to map from MRI to the ultrasound. A color example of this tissue mapping is shown in Fig. 4.
An example of different tissue types, as determined by the contrasted pixels in the sample breast MRI. Each tissue type is grouped by color

A near-surface boundary to the breast is detected using the tangent-line approach described in the previous section. Across these tangent vectors, we propagate a theoretical ultrasound beam to generate a localized, ultrasound-like tile.

We simulate ultrasound A-lines (one-dimensional echo amplitude vs. depth along a single ray) on a linear-array geometry. Adjacent A-lines sampled at successive lateral positions are stacked to form a rectangular B-mode in native depth × beam coordinates. In our Results section, we analyze image quality metrics in this native domain to avoid interpolation artifacts introduced by scan conversion.

To avoid air, we offset the beam by a few millimeters. We compute echo contributions every 0.3 mm (0.03 cm) along the beam, until 21 cm depth. 0.3 mm steps give detailed sampling, and allow for the resulting speckle to look natural. These steps yielded 700 samples, and along each depth sample the echo was calculated. At each depth sample k, we estimate the echo from the impedance change between adjacent points using the following equation:
Where  is included to avoid division by zero.

Ultrasound energy is lost continuously as it travels through tissue. In order to account for this, we apply attenuation that accumulates at each depth sample. Amplitude attenuation was applied per step using a two-way, dB-based Beer–Lambert model, defined by:
Where the product of  (MHz) and  (dB/cm/MHz) tells us the dB loss per centimeter.
We also added a small, tissue-dependent speckle texture by blurring noise (in a 5 × 5 kernel) so neighboring pixels look related. The generated synthetic ultrasound is shown in Fig. 5.
(Left) Schematic of the parallel A-line beam propagation through the breast tissue, (Right) Synthetic rectangular B-mode formed by stacking A-lines in depth × beam coordinates
Results

Because there are no current open-source paired MRI and US datasets on which to test our simulator, we had to turn to other statistical measures to validate our results. We selected four tests that correspond to routine ultrasound quality-assurance measures: depth mapping and reflectivity, attenuation with depth, spatial resolution, and low-contrast detectability. Together they verify trend-correct behavior without requiring a full scanner model. Table 2 summarizes the results and the metrics that we used to quantify the accuracy of the propagation model.
Summary of Metrics

(1) Planar interface: Because ultrasounds turn time into depth, this test is important because it determines the depth accuracy. Depth accuracy looks at whether the echo time maps to the correct depth. For this calculation, we defined a simple gel-tissue boundary at 5.00 cm and noted whether the simulator reports the echo at the right location and with the right strength. The envelope peak describes the depth accuracy, and appeared at 4.56 cm, meaning it was off by 0.44 cm. This indicates a small range offset; the echo is present and sharp, but shifted slightly.

This test also looks at the interface strength, which confirms that echoes appear at the right depth and with the right amplitude for a single flat gel–tissue boundary. To do this, we calculate the Fresnel reflectivity, which is a description of how much acoustics reflect off a surface at the boundary between two different materials. The Fresnel reflectivity is defined as:

At a flat gel to tissue boundary, the is 0.026. This value is the theoretical interface amplitude, and our value should match this value. Our measured pre-normalization amplitude matched this prediction. This shows that our model reproduces the expected echo strength from the impedance contrast.

(2) Axial attenuation fit: Ultrasound echoes get weaker with depth because tissue absorbs and scatters sound. This is the physical reason the ultrasound images darken as the probe looks deeper. For a uniform medium with attenuation of 0.70 dB/cm/MHz and a 4.5-MHz probe, theoretical math predicts a depth fall-off of about 6.3 dB/cm. In our simulation, the lateral-mean echo level did decline with depth, but the fitted slope was 1.42 dB/cm, shown in Fig. 6. Attenuation slopes were computed from the envelope signal prior to log compression; logarithmic compression was applied only for display of B-mode images. This means the model captures the correct trend, but underestimates how quickly echoes should fade. This bias is expected with our simplified propagation, which has straight rays and single scattering. A fixed calibration could potentially align the fitted slope with the theoretical target.

Each curve shows the attenuation our simulator estimates versus the value we programmed for three probe frequencies (3.5, 5.0, 7.5 MHz). The gray dashed line is “perfect agreement.” The estimates correctly move up or down as true attenuation changes, but they sit below the dashed line (especially at higher frequency) meaning the model under-calls absolute attenuation. This is expected for our simplified physics and gain processing and can be corrected with a simple calibration. Clinically, this means depth brightness drops in the right direction, but absolute numbers are conservative

Axial full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) measured in the simulator as a function of center frequency for three programmed attenuation levels. Higher frequency generally gives sharper axial detail (smaller FWHM). Because the current model does not explicitly simulate transmit pulse bandwidth or transducer impulse response, frequency-dependent changes in axial resolution are weak and not strictly monotonic; attenuation-driven effects dominate. This figure therefore reports empirical simulator behavior rather than ideal scanner physics

(3) Point-spread function: This test describes our simulator’s blur pattern, characterizing the intrinsic sharpness of the system. A distribution of this is shown in Fig. 7. Two nearby structures will only look separate only if the blur from one structure does not blur into the adjacent structure. We quantify that blur with the Full Width At Half Maximum (FWHM) score. Axial FWHM describes the separability of structure that are stacked one behind another, while Lateral FWHM describes the separability of side-by-side structures. A smaller FWHM score indicates sharper results. In a full ultrasound system, axial resolution is governed primarily by pulse bandwidth rather than center frequency alone. Because our simplified simulator does not yet model transducer impulse response or bandwidth, the frequency parameter here affects attenuation more strongly than pulse length, leading to non-monotonic trends in axial FWHM.
Under nominal settings, the axial FWHM was 6.30 mm and the lateral FWHM was 15.32 mm.

The lateral value is broader because we do not model beamforming or dynamic focusing. With these settings, structures closer than 6.30 mm axially or 15.32 mm laterally will merge on the simulated image.

(4) Cyst contrast and Contrast-to-Noise-Ratio (CNR): Acting as the most clinically important of the tests, this test looks at whether the simulator preserves clinically relevant contrast behavior when transforming from MRI to ultrasound. A clinically useful image must show a dark, low-contrast region against speckle. To test this, we placed a simple fake cyst (a “cyst phantom”), which is an anechoic disk inside a speckled background. We used this cyst to measure contrast, which is the difference in mean brightness, and CNR, which is the difference divided by background noise.

An anechoic disk embedded in speckle produced a contrast value of 0.34 and a CNR of 0.37 at nominal settings. As expected, increasing attenuation reduced both metrics, and increasing depth-wise gain improved them. This shows that the simulator preserves the expected relationship between tissue gain and lesion visibility, which is the behavior clinicians rely on during scanning.

Overall, the simulator reproduced first-order ultrasound trends. Boundaries appeared at the right depth and with the expected echo strength. These echoes became dimmer with depth, and higher-frequency probes gave crisper detail in the beam direction but less penetration. Side-to-side image sharpness in adjacent tissues remained broader because we did not model clinical beamforming. Some absolute numbers (such as the specific rate of dimming) were flatter than a full scanner, but the directions and relative sizes of the effects were accurate. Taken all together results support using the simulator to generate synthetic B-mode images. These results could be improved with more detailed calibration of the model.
Discussion

Despite using a simplified physics model, the simulator reproduced the fundamental behaviors clinicians rely on during ultrasound interpretation. A known boundary appeared at the expected depth with echo intensity proportional to the acoustic impedance mismatch between tissues. We find that the echoes attenuated appropriately with depth, and hypoechoic cysts maintained their characteristic appearance. Critically, the model captures the directional trends of key ultrasound physics, which form the foundation of clinical interpretation.

Some quantitative differences from clinical scanners are expected and arise from known simplifications in the current implementation. The model traces straight beams and accounts for single-scattering events without incorporating refraction, dynamic focusing, or multiple scattering. Preprocessing steps that normalize brightness across depth also flatten the apparent attenuation curve. Lateral resolution is better than clinical systems, with superior signal-to-noise ratios even at deep penetration depths. These are systematic deviations that stem from known design simplifications rather than fundamental design flaws, making them addressable through calibration or targeted design enhancements.

These findings directly address the clinical translation gap that plagues AI-driven medical imaging. Current deep learning models are built on an extractive paradigm; we mine datasets for statistical patterns, produce diagnostic outputs, and treat the process as complete. This linear approach can be less accurate when applied to different patient populations over which the data was not originally trained. When machine learning models encounter scanners, protocols, or patient populations that differ from their training data, they often fail. This lack of generalizability and the model’s inability to maintain performance across diverse settings severely limits clinical adoption of these techniques. Our work identifies the root causes of these failures and develops solutions to improve model robustness in real-world medical environments.

Rather than treating MRI synthesis as a black-box statistical problem, we offer a solution to embed the model within the physical principles that actually generate MRI signals. Because of this, it produces training examples with real clinical meaning, which may help decrease statistical artifacts generated by many image generation models. The synthetic images preserve anatomical fidelity because they emerge from physical relationships, not learned correlations.

This matters clinically in three concrete ways: First, it solves the generalization problem that keeps artificial intelligence trapped in research labs rather than clinical practice. Second, it addresses the scarcity of paired training data, a resource we cannot easily extract more of without imposing additional burden on patients. Third, it creates interpretable outputs that align with how clinicians actually understand MRI physics. This builds trust rather than demanding blind faith in algorithmic predictions. In essence, this study shows how we can improve artificial intelligence models into a system that can expand and adapt to the problems that are set to it.

Future refinements will enhance clinical fidelity and build more complex systems that accounts for various other artifacts that are present in real ultrasounds. Planned additions include spatially varying sound speed with refraction modeling and calibrated noise and speckle models. We also plan to model the shape and focusing of the ultrasound pulse, which will make frequency-related resolution behavior more realistic, thereby creating a model that is closer to an ideal scanner. This pursuit of a more ideal scanner is furthered by rendering a sector fan from a point apex with scan conversion. This will included in future work aimed at scanner-style visualization. These are incremental enhancements that maintain the transparent relationship between model parameters and image features, which remains a requirement for clinical deployment.
Conclusion

We present a compact, interpretable simulator that transforms MRI data into synthetic ultrasound images through physically grounded acoustic modeling. The system converts an MRI slice into acoustic property maps, automatically detects the skin contour, launches a fan of acoustic beams with physics-based attenuation and pulse propagation, and performs envelope detection with logarithmic compression to generate B-mode images. Validation tests confirm that the simulator reproduces expected ultrasound behaviors, though some absolute values differ from full clinical systems in predictable ways.

Breast MRI’s major diagnostic advantage commonly arises from DCE sequences after gadolinium administration, which encode perfusion and permeability rather than purely anatomic intensity. Our simulator does not incorporate vascular compartments and therefore cannot reproduce such patterns on DCE-MRI. Similarly, this work does not model contrast-enhanced ultrasound agents or small-molecule agents that may extravasate through leaky tumor neovasculature. Extending the framework to incorporate vascular maps and time-dependent contrast kinetics is an important direction for future work.

This tool addresses an immediate need in clinical research by enabling controlled generation of paired datasets when acquiring paired MRI–ultrasound data is impractical. It supports the benchmarking of cross-modal synthesis methods and facilitates the development of more robust machine learning models through physics-informed training data. By maintaining transparency at every processing step, this simulator provides a validation framework that clinicians and researchers can use to not only build better models, but understand them. This establishes a practical path toward increasingly realistic synthetic ultrasound generation without sacrificing the interpretability essential for clinical translation.
